Narrative_Meaning: Twos signify conflict, opposition and choices. They can indicate crossroads, partings, separations, and changes of direction.

Yarn.Yarn_Description: The Swamp is a card the represents a dichotomy. The Swamp is to be feared, it is a place of corruption, decay, stagnation, and many believe it magical, for those who enter it unprepared will die, but for those that know its secret ways, recognise its dangers, then medicinal plants, herbs, and other medicines and magics are revealed. The Swamp can represent potions and medicines that are as foul-tasting as swamp-water, and yet somehow help.

Significator.Plot: The Swamp Plot is about corruption and usually that means business and politics. It may be about crime, dirt, and sin in general, but usually this is a story of greed and power. Occasionally, a Swamp plot will actually be set in a real swamp, but more often it is just a Location, but is treated more like a Character, often fulfilling a Character Role such as love interest or rival.
Significator.Side: A side marked by The Swamp is marked as corrupted and degenerate some how. Sometimes they are in-bred cannibals, other times they are dirty cops, occasionally they can be redeemed.
Significator.Character:
The Swamp Significator indicates a Character who is corrupt, but powerful.

They Gain Yin when try to corrupt or tempt another.

They Gain Yang when they act selfishly or for themselves.

They Gain Chi when they demonstrate their power (use their Power or Super-Annex).

They also gain +1 Success Levels when they are performing immoral or illegal actions.
Significator.Diegetic_Character: A potent but morally compromised figure, whose influence is rooted in corruption and self-interest. They are masters of temptation and exploitation, thriving in environments of decay and finding their greatest strength when operating outside the bounds of traditional morality.
Significator.Scene: The Swamp Scene is usually one of despair or malaise. Swamps when they literally appear are a struggle, at the best of times. Swamps can be beautiful places, but the light has to be just right... Yarn-Tellers may play the card to engage in a little light corruption, make a Gain, or slap an Ordeal replete with Festering Wounds on a Character.

Yarn.Aspect_Description: The Hook targets the Character's Hitches, the bigger the better.
Yarn.Hook_Description: The Story Hooks the Characters with a direct warning and a question, this is usually what's that smell? But it can be What's that noise, taste or weird light, just as easily. In any case the answer is usually a corpse, occasionally a Monster, and just occasionally a broken sewer.
Yarn.Gain: Dark Territory or Dark Matter Descendant
Yarn.Diegetic_Gain: Control over a desolate or corrupted region, or the acquisition of a substance that defies natural laws, providing a dark and dangerous source of power.
Yarn.Fray_Description: A Quagmire is a sticky morass or corruption which the PCs find themselves engulfed by. Quagmires can be literal swamps, so called Quicksand, and so on, or more likely they can be sticky situations, economic quagmires are rising debts, negative equity and falling wages, political quagmires are usually the opposite, with money from all sorts of dubious sources flooding in, who only want small concessions and favours. Either way, the PCs will need to extricate themselves somehow.
Yarn.Snag_Description: The Snag adds the complication to the Fray that the situation or Location is truly unique. It might be that the ingredients for the ritual required grow only in one place, that must be visited, or that the nightclub has the only indoor grass dance floor in the city. It is usual for the unique situation to either threaten the PCs or be threatened by the Opposed Embodiments.

NPC.Description: The Witch (or less commonly Witch-Doctor) is a often a bitter outcast from society, who has learned the ways of the swamp, and the medicines found there. Sometimes they may be persuaded, paid or manipulated into helping others with their knowledge, often though they use their gifts to selfish ends. The Witch(-Doctor) is often accused of being able to fly or astral project themselves, and certainly can use herbs and plants to undergo psychotropic journeys. Some will have a Shaman Persona instead and be better socially adjusted.

Yarn.Sweeping_Text: The Shower Scene may not take place in a Shower or Bath, but represents the PCs trying to scrub free whatever they feel still clings to them. This may be some mud, or it could be an emotional taint. The Sweeping Scene allows the Character to treat their own wounds (especially Psych Wounds) with some ease (Halved Difficulty usually).
Yarn.History_Description: The Swamp indicates that the current situation is a corruption of what was before, there will be some similarities left, but this situation is a dark reflection in muddy waters of what came before.
Yarn.Situation_Description: The Swamp indicates a situation where you are caught somehow, often in a dangerous place. Characters can be mired by debt, by fear, by threats or actual violence, most often there is abuse and corruption around these circumstances.
Yarn.Solution_Description: The Swamp indicates that the solution to the current situation is a potion, which may be magical, medicinal, alcoholic, herbal, curative, miraculous or occasionally poisonous. Sometimes this potion may actually be a pill, herb or antidote, and just occasionally it is just a beverage.

Revelations.About_Description: The information is about the Pressed Conflict Embodiment, which is often a PC in a game, and is usually the antagonist in a Story.
Revelations.Info_Description: Corruption can range from a little bribery to actual demonic influences, and everything in between. Finding out about corruption is easy, although rarely will you get the details.
Revelations.Vector_Description: Scents can tell us a lot, reminding us of places, people, animals and of course events. The lingering scent of gun-smoke or fire can pass more than enough information for action.
Revelations.Alternate_Description: The information is about the influences that are working in the plot, or on the Character. These might be just the politics of their social media friends, the meddlings of some Seelie Noble, or the machinations of an Increated, you just never know.
Revelations.Detail_Description: Critical details are either spotting flaws in an otherwise perfect planet killer space base, or a critique that one can use to improve one's performance.

Obstacle.Description: The way ahead is repulsive, icky or disgusting. A Swamp, sewer, river of blood, pile of manure, shower of vomit. Strong nerves, stronger stomachs and the strongest immune systems will be required.

Tarot.Crossed: Automatic Proficiency Crisis. You can add a Proficiency to, or create, an Annex (if you have a slot free).
Tarot.Normal: Reroll any dice including a Proficiency Crisis.

Ordeal.Stage_Description: The route ahead is dangerous, littered with sharp stones, broken glass, or even landmines.
Ordeal.Motional_Description: Hit the gas! They're right behind us!
Ordeal.Tide_of_Battle_Text: The Ebbing enemy are at breaking point, one good assault should see them done. The Flowing force need only cause more Wounds than they take this Stage to win the round.
Ordeal.Fight_Description:
A Scare is a sudden Psych attack (such as a Duck attack), from a Vex or Chorus Monster that quickly retreats.
  Facet    Monster  
Gossamer ⚊ ♠

Suit: Spades ♠

Ordeal Trump: Attack / Perception / Penetration (Opening / Navigation / Logic)

Description: The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. Competition, perception, endurance and curiosity are positive aspects associated with the suit, but spades also represent destruction, betrayal, distance, might and death. In the tarot, the Suit is replaced with Swords, which (depending on the tradition) may represent either damaging Fire, or more commonly the cutting intellect of Air.

Tarot Meaning: Misfortune, Suffering, Loss, Mourning, Treachery, Betrayal, War, Lawsuit, Conflict.

Ordeal Text: Ordeal Types: Confrontation (e.g. Warfare, Lawsuit), Competition (Sports, Quizzes etc), Perception (Spot Hidden). Spades act as Trumps for the Following Action Types: Attacking, Balance, Climbing, Contagion, Desecration, Destruction, Feats of Strength, Lock-picking, Navigation, Perception (Search/Spot), Staying power, and Stoicism. Spades can be used for any type of attack.

Parry:Redirect-AttackThe damage from the original attack (less the Parried Card) is redirected by the Defender to the Attacker, their Descendants or their Allies.

Soak: Counter-AttackThe matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate.

Wiry — The Monster is immensely physically powerful, although often its form may not reveal it. It often demonstrates supernatural power as well as strength and toughness. A small, strong Goblin, a robot that looks like a little girl, a cybernetically enhanced rat, or even a killer doll (when not an Object Monster) are all examples of a Gossamer Monster. The Wiry Monster can add Gossamer Score cards to its Monster Pool whenever it is underestimated, cared for, or demonstrates its strength. A Wiry monster may add their current Monster Pool Size Score (Number Reduced) Success Levels to any action with their strength or toughness. Wiries must spend cards from their Monster Pool as though it was a Prepared Defence to ignore damage.
Inertia ⚋ ♠

Suit: Spades ♠

Ordeal Trump: Attack / Perception / Penetration (Opening / Navigation / Logic)

Spades can be used for any type of attack.

Soak: Counter-AttackThe matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate.

Unstoppable — The Monster is inexorable, it does not stop and cannot be killed by any normal means. They start play with a Monster Pool of Inertia Score cards. Unstoppable Monsters can add Inertia Draw cards to their Monster Pool whenever they cross a threshold, pass a barrier, or are forced to stop or turn around. Unstoppable Monsters can ignore a number of Wounds equal to the number of cards in their Monster Pool. Wounds stacked beyond the Monster Pool affect them as normal. Inexorable Monsters can also ignore all Hitches with a Boon less than their Monster Pool Size. Whenever an Unstoppable Monster is damaged beyond this limit, it must Discard the Highest Card in the Monster Pool, it may immediately also Discard any Wounds lower than discarded Monster Card, any remaining Wounds are then discarded on a one Monster Pool card per Wound basis. However, while discarding these wounds and cards the Monster must remain still (playing dead) until a Chi Extend Duration equal to the Number of Monster Pool cards Discarded (the initial card is instant, the next discard takes an action, then a second takes a few seconds, the third Wound takes an Ordeal Round, 25 Wounds would take a week). Examples of the Inertial Monster type include the Hungry Dead (Zombies) and most robotic assassins (especially ones from the future).
Key ⚊ ♠

Suit: Spades ♠

Ordeal Trump: Attack / Perception / Penetration (Opening / Navigation / Logic)

Hunter — The Monster is a hunter or tracker, it should favour harrying attacks and hit and run tactics. Typically such a Monster will vanish or flee if it is wounded, only to reappear when healed. The hunter may be fooled by false trails, but never for long. Hunters may add Key Draw cards to their Monster Pool whenever they spot a track, clue, or similar, that indicates the subject of their hunt. Hunters receive a bonus to their Score or Pips equal to the number of cards in the Monster Pool on any tracking, attacking or hunting actions if they also spend a card from the Monster Pool on the action. Hunters may convert Mortal and Carnage Wounds that they take into Crippling Wounds instead, at a cost of one card from their Monster Pool. In T13, the archetypal version of this Monster is a Bulmäs Pack member from the Wild Hunt, but any Werewolf or Animal Shifter would probably qualify, as would robot cowboys in theme parks that have gone wrong, and any fantasy world’s Elven ranger.
Miasma ⚋ ♠

Suit: Spades ♠

Ordeal Trump: Attack / Perception / Penetration (Opening / Navigation / Logic)

Description: The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. In the tarot, the Suit is replaced with Swords, which (depending on the tradition) may represent either damaging Fire, or more commonly the cutting intellect of Air.

Soak: Counter-AttackThe matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate.

Mutant — The monster is a mutant or chimerical creature made from parts of others. This monster type is the oldest perhaps, you’ll find examples throughout the ancient world, particularly in Greek myth. The Mutant will often be larger, and may be malformed or unsymmetrical, with odd numbers of limbs, eyes, horns, or even heads. The Chimera will blend multiple animals together to create a new creature that combines features of all the “parent” creatures, preserving the best of all species. Mutant Monsters start with Miasma Score Monster Pool. They may add Miasma Draw cards to the Pool every time they accomplish a specified goal, such as eating a human, consuming a soul, bathing in radiation, or just growing a year older, etc. The Monster Pool can be used as a “Mutation” Annex, which can be used to mimic the abilities of the Mutants “parent” creatures, such as a spider’s ability to climb, or an eagle’s ability to fly. The Mutation Annex has a Boon equal to the Value of the Number of Cards in the Monster Pool, and so draws cards equal to the Monster Pool Size Reduced and plays a number of cards equal to the Miasma Draw. The Mutation Annex is often used as a Size Annex for the Mutant. Where a 5 card Monster Pool would create a Boon 16 Annex, and could grant a Kaiju Mutant the size of a Skyscraper. 1950s B-movie monsters are often of this type, giant ants, lizards, spiders, wasps, and even rabbits have been used. Classically of course, Cyclopses, Centaurs, Minotaurs, Satyrs, Gryphons (or Griffins), Hippogriffs, Dragons, Sphinxes, Manticores and of course the Chimera itself are all examples. You can also find examples in superheroes, especially those created by radioactive arachnids, or those with an X-gene.
Trial ⚊ ♠

Suit: Spades ♠

Ordeal Trump: Attack / Perception / Penetration (Opening / Navigation / Logic)

Soak: Counter-AttackThe matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate.

Killer — The Killer Monster is a deadly Combat specialist of some sort. Killers add Trial Draw cards to their Monster Pool each time they kill. Killers automatically add +1 Success Level per Monster Pool Size Score (Number Reduced) to all attacks, and Monster Pool Size Score (Number Reduced) is automatically added to the Pips of any attack as Penetration. Killers must spend Monster Pool cards whenever they will make a Trick either with other cards being played by the Ordeal Pool, or other cards in the Round Pool or a Style Reserve. Killers can be drug-addled assassins, crazed shooters, or just steel-souled soldiers.
Yonder ⚋ ♠

Suit: Spades ♠

Ordeal Trump: Attack / Perception / Penetration (Opening / Navigation / Logic)

Description: The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. Alien — The Monster is an alien, an outsider. It may just be a metaphor for alienation, or literally be an extra-(or ultra)-terrestrial. Such monsters are usually obvious and inhuman, not just to look at, but in the way they think and behave. They may have apparently supernatural powers and abilities, but often make do with advanced technologies (in the form of additional Descendants). They may add Yonder Draw cards to their Monster Pool when they are observed doing their alien / inhuman / weird actions. They may add Monster Pool Size Reduced Success Levels to any action to do with their alien abilities, technologies, motion or survival (including Dodge, Soak, etc). Alien Monsters must play cards from their Monster Pool to socialise with “non-aliens”. From little grey Eusaiveans, through pod people and pepper-pot shaped engines of hate, all the way to face-hugging space-wasps, there are plenty of aliens.
  Facet    Monster

Lea.Notes: Microscopic viral and bacterial paths, often overlooked, this can mimic any larger animal paths.

Stress.Description: Worries make a Character think about their issues, dwelling on what questions or issues are stressing them out.
Stress.Rules: Character may reduce their Stress by 1 for each statement they make, that relates to their worries, character that they talk to about it, or they may pay Chi equal to their current Stress as Tao Sway to remove 1 Stress.

Trauma.Description: Trauma can twist a Character, easily making personality traits and behaviours more toxic. This toxicity will usually express in passive-aggressions, unwarranted criticism of others, snide remarks, sarcastic attitudes, elaborate excuses for their behaviour, seeking to blame others for any failures, manipulating others for their own ends.
Trauma.Rules: The Trauma instantly Relieves all current Stress, which is used to purchase a "Toxic" Hitch, that will typically interfere with relationships, create bad impressions and hamper the Character socially. The Toxic Character can Relieve 1 Stress by acting toxic somehow to another.

Age.Description: An Age of Corruption is occurring. During this age society begins to break down, individual members of the society behave in their own self-interest, rather than the interest of their society. Corruption, extortion, exploitation, fraud, profiteering, racketeering, malfeasence and nepotism are the order of the day, often with a hint of social injustice and base criminality. A corrupt society does not serve its membership and they will often form new societies, abandonning their old, where they cannot fix the society's ills.
Age.Diegetic_Description: A dark era of decay and self-interest, where the foundations of society are eroded by greed and malpractice. Trust is a rare commodity as corruption and exploitation become the norm, leading to a fragmented culture where individuals look only to their own gain and the collective good is forgotten.
Age.Rules: During an Age of Corruption most members turn aside from the society, whether to exploit the other members, make a quick buck, or to excape the corruption within the society. This allows exploitation narratives and corruption stories to Embody within the society. Allowing an age of corruption to continue unchecked always has a negative effect upon the society, costing them membership to other nearby, and less corrupt societies, and often leading to a complete collapse.
